includedSources: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side of the Boom (1)
skippedSources: McKinsey & Company — 'How Covista is changing education' (18:53 CEST): pure corporate-brand teaser, zero sentences of analytical content beyond a one-line synopsis
Substack — 'New follower' notification (16:01 CEST): automated notification, no content
Ecoinometrics — 'Bitcoin Is Starting To Stabilize' (13:30 CEST): paywalled preview cuts off before any data point or claim is made

items.title: Capability 10x'd in 2 years. Reliability moved 5-10 points. Same benchmark number covers both.
items.idea: Narayanan's team benchmarked frontier models from three labs over the last 24 months on capability AND reliability (consistency, robustness, calibration, operational safety) separately. Capability shot up dramatically; reliability crept up only 5-10 percentage points. Almost no agent benchmark distinguishes '70% accuracy = works on 70% of tasks every time' from '70% accuracy = fails a coin-flip on every single task' — yet those are opposite deployment realities.
items.angle: This is the missing variable in the AI-capex-vs-adoption argument that Goldman's 'debt bubble' note and the 200-economist jobs letter are both dancing around this week — nobody's pricing reliability, only capability.
items.tweetA: Frontier model capability roughly 10x'd in 2 years across 3 labs. Reliability moved 5-10 points. Every published benchmark reports both under the same 'accuracy' number — meaning the industry literally cannot tell you if a '70% accurate' agent is deployable or a coin flip.
items.tweetB: Goldman says AI debt bubble. 200 economists say AI jobs crisis. Both arguments assume capability = deployment. Narayanan's data says the actual gate is reliability, and reliability has barely moved. That gap, not the capability curve, is where 2027's alpha sits.

charts.desc: Capability vs reliability lines diverging over 24 months across 3 frontier labs — the accuracy-vs-reliability split most benchmarks never show.

items.title: Software engineering employment grew 10,000x through every 'automating' wave. Radiology grew after Hinton called it dead.
items.idea: Narayanan's decide/execute/deliver framework: AI is compressing only the 'execute' layer (coding/debugging), which was already ~1/3 of the job. Decide (requirements, planning) and deliver (integration, accountability, maintenance) aren't compressing — they're expanding. Hinton predicted radiologists extinct within 5 years a decade ago; radiology employment has grown since, because cheaper/faster tasks generate more demand for them (Jevons' paradox), not less.
items.angle: Every 'AI kills the job' forecast this week (the 200-economist letter included) is implicitly betting against a pattern that has held for ATMs, radiology, legal filings, and translation. The bet isn't crazy, but it's contrarian to the entire empirical record.
items.tweetA: Hinton said radiologists would be extinct in 5 years. That was a decade ago. Radiology employment has grown since. The 200 economists warning about AI jobs this week are making the same structural bet Hinton made — and the base rate says it's 0-for-4 (ATMs, radiology, law, translation).
items.tweetB: AI is compressing 'execute' (coding, debugging) — call it a third of a software engineer's job. 'Decide' and 'deliver' aren't shrinking, they're expanding to absorb the slack. Ten-times-more-productive engineers didn't mean ten-times-fewer engineers last time either: employment grew 10,000x through every prior 10x productivity wave.

charts.desc: No chart — framework slide only, described verbally in the talk transcript.

items.title: 80% of the world's software runs on open source. AI is now flooding it with code nobody's paid to review.
items.idea: Vibe coding lets non-programmers ship software, but open-source maintainers — mostly unpaid volunteers — are now getting flooded with AI-generated pull requests they have to vet with zero compute budget, while closed-source companies fight AI-generated code with AI-agent review at scale. The asymmetry: well-funded labs can review at machine speed, the substrate everything else runs on can't.
items.angle: This is a supply-chain-security story hiding inside an AI-adoption story. Every SaaS company running on open-source infra is exposed to unvetted-by-humans code with no analogous compliance framework yet.
items.tweetA: Closed-source AI labs fight AI-generated code with AI-agent code review at scale. The open-source maintainers holding up 80% of the software you run today have zero compute budget to do the same. This is a supply-chain risk nobody's pricing.
items.tweetB: Vibe coding didn't create a security problem. It removed the rate-limiter on an existing one. Open source always relied on volunteer review capacity — that capacity was fixed, and submission volume just went vertical.

charts.desc: No chart in email; FT source article likely has visuals — worth opening.

items.title: 200 economists, several Nobel laureates, warn AI jobs losses outrun benefits. Anthropic's Jack Clark leads the letter.
items.idea: Parekh's pushback: the models can forecast the X jobs AI destroys, but structurally cannot measure the Z jobs that didn't exist yet — the new categories of work that show up only after the technology diffuses. He notes the letter burnishes Anthropic's safety brand even as Anthropic is the company racing hardest on capability, and draws the parallel to early-1900s demands to cap car speeds at horse-speed.
items.angle: Worth cross-referencing against Narayanan's separate keynote the same day (item #2) — both land on the same critique of the jobs-loss models from completely independent angles, which makes the critique stronger, not weaker.
items.tweetA: 200 economists + Nobel laureates warn AI destroys jobs faster than benefits arrive. Same week, a Princeton AI-safety researcher runs the numbers and finds reliability — not capability — is the actual deployment bottleneck. Two unrelated sources, same conclusion: the jobs-loss models are pricing a capability curve that isn't the one that matters.
items.tweetB: Jack Clark (Anthropic) leads a letter warning AI could destroy jobs faster than benefits arrive — while Anthropic races capability as hard as anyone. The letter is good PR and might also be right. Those aren't mutually exclusive, but it's worth noticing the incentive sits on both sides.

charts.desc: No chart; NYT source article referenced.

items.title: 20-30 model variants per lab before you've picked a task. Devs post 90-minute videos just to choose one.
items.idea: Each frontier lab now ships 3-4 sizes x 3-4 intensities per model — 20-30 configurations before you've even started the task. Advanced users are watching Pareto curves of token cost, with bills running into the tens of thousands per day for developer-heavy use. Parekh's read: labs are optimizing a la carte, tiered pricing against scarce compute ahead of OpenAI's and Anthropic's expected mega-IPOs.
items.angle: This is a hidden tax on the highest-intent users right as the labs need revenue-per-user data to look good for IPO roadshows — the complexity itself may be a monetization strategy, not an accident.
items.tweetA: 20-30 model configurations per lab before you've picked a task. Developers are burning as much time optimizing which GPT-5.6 variant to run as they save using it. This isn't UX debt — it's a la carte pricing complexity engineered right before two AI labs' IPOs, when ARPU needs to look good.
items.tweetB: The AI cost curve everyone quotes ('$X per million tokens, falling fast') hides a pricing maze: 20-30 variants, tiered intensities, bills running tens of thousands a day for power users. The headline cost-per-token number and the actual bill a heavy user pays are two different stories.

charts.desc: No chart; Axios/Theo (t3.gg) source pieces referenced, likely have cost-comparison tables.

items.title: USB-C was invented by one engineer trying to fix his wife's printer cable. Now nobody can tell what any cable actually does.
items.idea: USB-C was born at Intel from an engineer solving a personal problem, then built out as an open standard by thousands of companies with no single owner — which is exactly why there's still no unified way to know a given cable's power/data/charging capability just by looking at it. The fix so far is a third-party Mac app, not a platform-level standard from Apple or Microsoft.
items.angle: A clean case study in how 'nobody owns the standard' produces decades-long usability debt even in objectively solved hardware — worth remembering next time someone claims an open standard is automatically the better outcome.
items.tweetA: USB-C: invented by one Intel engineer fixing his wife's printer cable, built out by thousands of companies, owned by none of them. Result: 15 years later there's still no way to tell what a cable does without plugging it in and hoping. Open standards solve adoption, not usability.
items.tweetB: A $12.99 app now exists because Apple and Microsoft still haven't shipped 'tell me what this cable can do' as an OS feature. If you want a proxy for how slowly platform incumbents ship unsexy fixes, this is a good one.

charts.desc: No chart; The Verge / The Atlantic source pieces referenced.

items.title: Vanguard — the firm that refused Bitcoin ETFs in Jan 2024 — is now hiring a Head of Digital Assets.
items.idea: Vanguard, $12T AUM, 50M+ investors, was the industry holdout on spot Bitcoin ETFs while BlackRock and Fidelity captured tens of billions in flows. It quietly allowed third-party crypto ETFs onto its platform by end-2025, and this week posted a job for a 'Head of Digital Assets' to lead Vanguard Personal Wealth's crypto strategy — while Bitcoin is still down ~30% YTD and gold down ~7%, the worst combined year for both assets on record.
items.angle: Institutional adoption is showing up in hiring decisions before it shows up in price — this is the same 'quiet infrastructure before the rally' pattern that preceded the 2023-24 ETF wave.
items.tweetA: Vanguard refused spot Bitcoin ETFs in Jan 2024 while BlackRock and Fidelity vacuumed up billions. Bitcoin is down 30% YTD, gold down 7% — worst combined year ever for both. And Vanguard is hiring a Head of Digital Assets right now. Institutions build the plumbing during the bear market, not the rally.
items.tweetB: BlackRock's Bitcoin ETF reportedly earns more revenue than its flagship S&P 500 fund. That's the number that got Vanguard's HR department typing up a job posting — not the price chart. Fee revenue beats price action as an institutional trigger every time.

charts.desc: No chart embedded; article links to Vanguard job posting and AUM sourcing.

items.title: The average US home cost 50 BTC in 2020. It costs about 5 BTC now.
items.idea: Fidelity Digital Assets data: home prices in dollars hit a record $409K (Redfin), up over $100K since 2020 — but priced in Bitcoin, the average home fell from ~50 BTC (Q1 2020) to ~5 BTC (Q1 2026), a 10x drop in the BTC-denominated price despite the dollar price rising. M2 money supply expanded ~40% between Feb 2020 and Apr 2022, which is doing a lot of the explanatory work here alongside housing shortage and rates.
items.angle: This is the cleanest 'the measuring stick is the story, not the asset' chart you'll see this month — useful shorthand for the fiat-debasement argument without needing to touch inflation-expectations data at all.
items.tweetA: Average US home: 50 BTC in 2020, ~5 BTC now. Home prices in dollars are at a record high. Both statements are true at once — because the dollar, not the house, is the thing that moved. That's the entire fiat-debasement argument in one chart, no CPI print required.
items.tweetB: M2 grew ~40% in 26 months starting Feb 2020. Housing (scarce, can't be printed) absorbed a lot of that. Bitcoin (scarce, can't be printed) absorbed a different slice. Comparing the two assets against each other cancels out the dollar and shows you what actually happened to purchasing power.

charts.desc: Fidelity Digital Assets chart: average US home price in BTC terms, 2020-2026 — the 50→5 BTC collapse.

items.title: Bitcoin's biggest governance fight since 2017's block-size wars is heating up — and miners aren't signaling support.
items.idea: BIP-110 would soft-fork a one-year limit on arbitrary data storage (inscriptions, tokens) on Bitcoin's chain. Signaling deadline is early August. Miner support sits under 1%; about 15% of nodes run BIP-110-compatible software. Michael Saylor and Lyn Alden have both weighed in publicly, with Alden questioning whether it addresses the underlying fee incentives at all.
items.angle: A governance fight this size, this early in signaling, with miner support this low, is a low-probability-but-high-impact tail risk for anyone holding BTC through August — chain-split risk is a real, if small, line item.
items.tweetA: BIP-110 signaling deadline is early August. Miner support: under 1%. Node support: ~15%. That's not a consensus fork building momentum, that's a fight that hasn't found its majority yet — and the 2017 block-size war is the template for how ugly this gets if it doesn't resolve cleanly.
items.tweetB: Lyn Alden's actual critique of BIP-110 isn't 'this is dangerous,' it's 'this doesn't fix the incentive that causes the problem it's trying to solve.' That's a more interesting objection than the block-size-war nostalgia most of the discourse is running on.

charts.desc: No chart; node/miner signaling stats cited as figures only.

items.title: Boeing turnaround thesis, +5% since March, hits the 'press or hold' decision point.
items.idea: Haymaker recommended Boeing in mid-March (~$210) as a bet on CEO Kelly Ortberg's 'fix first, produce later' reset; it's up ~5% to the low-$220s. Production is ramping, deliveries are beating, and free cash flow inflected positive — consensus targets (~$270) sit well above spot, so the update frames the question as whether to keep pressing or just hold for the cash-flow story.
items.angle: A working industrial turnaround with FCF inflection and a ~20% gap to consensus target is a different risk profile than most of this week's macro noise — worth flagging as one of the few 'boring compounding' theses in a week dominated by Hormuz and KOSPI headlines.
items.tweetA: Boeing FCF just inflected positive. Deliveries are beating. Consensus target is ~20% above spot. Everyone's trading Hormuz and KOSPI this week — the actual boring, working turnaround story is sitting one screen over.
items.tweetB: 'Fix first, produce later' is not a sexy thesis. It's also up 5% since March with FCF now positive, which is the least sexy possible way to be right. Sometimes the trade is just watching production numbers instead of the news cycle.

charts.desc: No chart; position P&L stated as text figures only.

items.title: Waller: another hot core inflation print this week and the Fed 'will need to consider tightening.'
items.idea: Fed Governor Chris Waller, in a speech released today, said explicitly that another hot core CPI reading this week would force the FOMC to consider tightening 'in the near term' — with the next meeting just two weeks out. Fed funds futures are now pricing a 50% chance of a hike at that meeting, a sharp repricing from a market that spent 2026 assuming cuts.
items.angle: This lands the same week David Rosenberg (separately, in-window) is framing Warsh's decision as whether to 'refute or validate' the bond market's pricing of two hikes — two Fed-watchers converging on the same live repricing from different vantage points, hours apart.
items.tweetA: Waller just told markets: one more hot core CPI print this week and a hike is 'near term.' Fed funds futures: 50% odds of a hike in two weeks. This isn't dovish-pivot positioning anymore — the market spent all of 2026 pricing cuts and just re-priced to coin-flip hikes off one speech.
items.tweetB: Two Fed-adjacent macro writers, same day, converging independently on 'the bond market is now pricing two hikes and Fed officials are deciding whether to validate it.' When Boockvar and Rosenberg land on the same repricing from different angles hours apart, that's not noise.

charts.desc: No chart; fed funds futures pricing cited as a figure only.

items.title: AI is already compressing film/TV preproduction timelines enough to greenlight shows that couldn't have existed a year ago.
items.idea: McKinsey's explainer (Jamie Vickers, Alec Wrubel) traces AI's impact across the full production pipeline — preproduction compression, automated postproduction workflows, and shows getting greenlit that wouldn't have cleared a budget/timeline bar twelve months ago.
items.angle: Thin on specifics, but the greenlighting claim is the tell: if AI is changing which projects clear the finance committee, not just how fast existing projects ship, that's a content-supply shock worth tracking into streamer earnings this year.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's claim buried in a video explainer: AI isn't just speeding up film/TV production, it's changing which shows get greenlit at all — projects that wouldn't have cleared budget or timeline hurdles a year ago now do. That's a supply-side shock to content volume, not just a cost story.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching AI video generation for the flashy demo. The quieter story is preproduction and postproduction workflow compression making previously-unfundable shows fundable. Follow the greenlight data, not the render quality.

charts.desc: Video explainer format, no static chart in email.

items.title: "Most people aren't trying to save time. They're trying to spend it."
items.idea: a16z's Anish A argues most technology of the last century was built to let people have more while doing less — but the thing people actually want (per Character.ai's Eugenia Kuyda, quoted directly) is to spend their time making things, not save it. He places AI alongside language, the printing press, and the steam engine as one of the rare technologies that both saves labor AND expands what a person can be.
items.angle: This reframes the entire 'AI saves you X hours' product-marketing category as measuring the wrong variable — the winning consumer AI products may be the ones that maximize hours spent, not hours saved, which is a completely different growth metric to underwrite.
items.tweetA: Every AI product pitch leads with hours saved. Kuyda's (Character.ai) actual insight, via a16z this week: people don't want time back, they want somewhere to spend it. If that's right, the consumer AI companies optimizing for 'time saved' are optimizing for the wrong KPI entirely — engagement-per-session beats efficiency-per-task.
items.tweetB: Language, the printing press, the steam engine, AI — a16z's list of technologies that saved labor AND expanded what a person could be. Notice what's missing from that four-item list: social media. The essay's actual thesis is that AI can go either way, and the difference is whether it's used to make or just to consume.

charts.desc: No chart; essay format.

items.title: A Kentucky electrician built a $12.99 tool that replaces a $500 service call. A plumber canceled a $40K consulting contract after one afternoon with AI.
items.idea: Two concrete a16z examples of AI-enabled disintermediation outside tech: a master electrician with no CS background built a load-calculation tool now selling for $12.99, undercutting a $500 service call; a plumber canceled a $40,000 consulting engagement after a single afternoon with an AI coding tool got him further than weeks of consultant scoping had.
items.angle: These are the real-economy labor-substitution data points the 200-economist jobs letter (item #4) is worried about — except the substitution here is a tradesperson displacing a consultant, not AI displacing the tradesperson. The disruption is happening one layer up the value chain from where most people are watching.
items.tweetA: A plumber canceled a $40,000 consulting contract after one afternoon with an AI coding tool. Nobody is modeling this in the 'AI job loss' letters — because the job that got automated wasn't the plumber's, it was the consultant's. The disruption is happening one layer up from where everyone's watching.
items.tweetB: $500 service call, replaced by a $12.99 app an electrician with no CS degree built himself. That's not a productivity statistic, that's a price collapse in a specific local service market. Multiply this pattern across every trade with a proprietary-knowledge tax and you get a very different inflation picture for skilled-trade services.

charts.desc: No chart; anecdotal examples in essay text.

items.title: USDT dominance is rolling over on a bearish divergence, and BTC/SPX just printed a weekly bullish divergence.
items.idea: Two chart-based signals flagged together: USDT dominance (a proxy for capital sitting in stablecoins rather than risk assets) is showing a solid bearish divergence that looks set to confirm; simultaneously the BTC/SPX ratio shows a weekly bullish divergence, which the author reads as an early signal that a stock-to-crypto rotation may finally be starting after a long drought.
items.angle: Two independent technical signals pointing the same direction (capital rotating out of stables, crypto starting to outperform equities) is a stronger setup than either alone — worth a small starter position sizing test rather than ignoring as noise.
items.tweetA: USDT dominance rolling over on a bearish divergence. BTC/SPX printing a weekly bullish divergence at the same time. Two independent charts, same conclusion: capital may be starting to rotate out of stables and into crypto relative to equities. Neither signal alone is a trade. Both together is worth a starter position.
items.tweetB: 'No narrative left' is the consensus complaint in crypto right now. The actual narrative, per this week's charts, is unglamorous: infinite money supply, finite coin supply, and a stablecoin-dominance chart quietly rolling over while nobody's watching.

charts.desc: USDT dominance chart showing bearish divergence; BTC/SPX weekly chart showing bullish divergence — both worth screenshotting.

items.title: Chinese wages have nearly doubled since 2016 — the 'cheap factory' narrative is running out of runway.
items.idea: A chart cited in this week's blocmates roundup shows Chinese wages have nearly doubled since 2016, directly undercutting the 'China as the world's low-cost factory floor' assumption that underpins a lot of reshoring/tariff/supply-chain thinking still circulating in Western commentary.
items.angle: If this trend holds, the economic logic behind 'China will always undercut on labor cost' needs updating — it changes the multi-year calculus for where marginal manufacturing capacity gets sited next, and interacts directly with the China-food-security and reshoring items elsewhere this week.
items.tweetA: Chinese wages have nearly doubled since 2016. Every 'China is the low-cost manufacturing floor' argument still floating around trade policy circles is running on a decade-stale assumption. The reshoring debate needs a wage chart, not a tariff chart.
items.tweetB: If Chinese labor keeps getting more expensive at this pace, the interesting question isn't 'does manufacturing leave China' — it's 'where does China's own capital go looking for the next cheap-labor frontier.' Vietnam, India, and parts of Africa are the second-order trade here.

charts.desc: Chart: Chinese average wages, 2016-2026, showing near-doubling.

items.title: The dollar's share of global FX reserves just hit its lowest level this century.
items.idea: A chart cited this week shows the US dollar's share of global foreign currency reserves has fallen to its lowest level since at least 2000 — a slow-moving de-dollarization data point sitting alongside the Vanguard crypto hire and the Bessent 'America will write the rules of the next economy' speech elsewhere in this week's flow.
items.angle: This is the multi-decade backdrop against which every stablecoin, CBDC, and digital-asset policy story this week (Vanguard, Circle's bank charter, BIP-110) is actually playing out — the reserve-currency erosion is the slow tectonic plate, the crypto headlines are the earthquakes on top of it.
items.tweetA: Dollar's share of global FX reserves: lowest this century. Same week Vanguard hires a crypto lead and Circle gets a federal bank charter. The de-dollarization story isn't a headline event, it's the slow-moving plate tectonics underneath every stablecoin and digital-asset policy story landing this week.
items.tweetB: Reserve managers don't dump the dollar in a headline moment — they just stop adding to it for a decade, and one day the share number is the lowest on record. This chart is the boring, unsexy version of the de-dollarization thesis, and it's also the more credible one.

charts.desc: Chart: USD share of global FX reserves over multi-decade history, new low.

items.title: AI investment now accounts for more than 25% of US GDP growth.
items.idea: Cited stat this week: AI-related capital investment now makes up more than a quarter of total US GDP growth — meaning the US economy's headline growth number is now materially dependent on AI capex continuing at pace, not just benefiting from it at the margin.
items.angle: Put this next to Goldman's 'AI debt bubble' note (referenced separately this week) and the Waller hike-pricing item (#11): if a quarter of GDP growth is AI capex and the Fed hikes into a capex slowdown, that's a much more direct transmission mechanism from Fed policy to headline GDP than the standard rate-hike-hits-consumer-spending story.
items.tweetA: AI investment is now more than 25% of US GDP growth. That means 'is the AI capex cycle sustainable' isn't a tech-sector question anymore, it's a headline-GDP question. A capex air pocket here doesn't just hit NVDA, it shows up in the next GDP print.
items.tweetB: Waller is talking about hiking into inflation. A quarter of GDP growth is AI capex. Put those two facts next to each other and the transmission mechanism from Fed policy to growth isn't 'consumers spend less,' it's 'hyperscalers slow capex,' which is a much more concentrated and much faster channel.

charts.desc: No chart embedded; figure cited as text stat.

items.title: SPY holds the 8-ema, but Korea keeps having 'terrible sessions' that ripple into US trading.
items.idea: Technical read: SPY remains above its 8-day EMA, so the short-term uptrend is intact per this author's framework. But he flags a new structural dynamic — Korean semiconductor-heavy indices have had multiple bad overnight sessions recently that are spilling into US trading, and he expects this Korea-to-US contagion channel to persist given how central Korean memory names have become to the broader market.
items.angle: This is a technician calling out, in real time, the exact KOSPI-to-US semiconductor contagion channel that bespokeinvest and neilsethi are independently documenting with hard data the same morning (items #27, #28) — three sources, one structural read, worth treating as more than coincidence.
items.tweetA: A pure technician, a data-driven morning note, and a global-markets roundup all flagged the same thing this morning independently: Korean memory-chip weakness is now a leading indicator for US sessions, not a regional curiosity. When three unrelated sources converge on the same contagion channel same-day, that's the signal.
items.tweetB: 'SPY above the 8-ema, uptrend intact' is doing a lot of work to paper over a KOSPI down 25% in three weeks. The index-level technical picture and the sector-level reality (Korean memory names) are telling two different stories right now — watch which one the index catches up to.

charts.desc: No chart; technical levels described in text.

items.title: 59% of US billion-dollar companies have an immigrant founder. DOL just launched its first major H-1B fraud probe — targeting the outsourcing model, not founders.
items.idea: NFAP data: 59% of US billion-dollar companies and 70% of Bay Area unicorns were founded or co-founded by immigrants. Same week, DOL's Inspector General launched, with DHS/DOJ under the White House Fraud Task Force, its first major multi-agency H-1B/PERM fraud investigation — dozens of subpoenas issued, Cognizant named. The probe targets wage-kickback schemes and the third-party staffing/outsourcing placement model specifically, which Mehta argues is structurally distinct from startups that directly employ their own engineering teams.
items.angle: The headline risk ('H-1B crackdown') and the actual enforcement target (outsourcing/staffing brokers) are two different things — worth distinguishing before assuming this touches the founder population responsible for the majority of unicorn creation.
items.tweetA: 59% of US billion-dollar companies have an immigrant founder. This week's H-1B fraud investigation targets wage-kickback schemes at outsourcing brokers, not founders who directly employ their own teams. The headline reads 'H-1B crackdown.' The subpoenas say 'staffing-model fraud.' Those are different stories and the market is conflating them.
items.tweetB: 70% of Bay Area unicorns have an immigrant founder. If the enforcement scope stays where the subpoenas currently point — third-party placement brokers — this is close to a non-event for the venture-funded startup population. If it expands, that's the thing to watch, not the headline itself.

charts.desc: No chart; stats cited as text figures.

items.title: EB-1 India green card line just retrogressed to October 2022 — and the State Department is warning it could go fully unavailable before September 30.
items.idea: The July 2026 Visa Bulletin pushed the EB-1 India Final Action Date back to October 15, 2022 — the primary green-card pathway for exceptional Indian-born founders. EB-2 India is already fully unavailable for the rest of FY2026. State Department has explicitly warned further retrogression, or full unavailability, remains possible before the fiscal year resets October 1.
items.angle: This is a direct talent-pipeline risk for any fund or company relying on Indian-origin technical talent on an EB-1/EB-2 track — worth flagging to portfolio companies now rather than after a further cutback lands.
items.tweetA: EB-1 India just retrogressed to an October 2022 priority date, and the State Department is explicitly warning it could go fully unavailable before September 30. If your portfolio companies have Indian-born technical co-founders on this track, this is not a 'someday' problem, it's a Q3 problem.
items.tweetB: EB-2 India: already dead for the rest of FY2026. EB-1 India: retrogressing, with an explicit warning of full closure ahead. The green-card pipeline for Indian-origin exceptional talent is narrowing in real time while everyone's attention is on H-1B headlines instead.

charts.desc: No chart; visa bulletin dates cited as text.

items.title: Founder used his own AI agent to raise his $100M round — the fundraise was the product demo.
items.idea: Lyzr AI closed a $100M Series B at ~$500M valuation, drawing over $400M in investor interest (4x the target) after deploying its own AI agent, SivaClaw, to handle much of the investor outreach, Q&A, and memo drafting during the raise itself. Founder Siva Surendira previously built and sold PowerUpCloud to L&T Infotech in 2019; Lyzr is now building governance infrastructure for regulated-industry AI agent deployment.
items.angle: This is the cleanest 'the pitch is the proof' story of the week — an enterprise AI governance company that used agentic AI to execute its own fundraise is a self-validating case study in a category (regulated-industry AI infra) that's otherwise hard to underwrite from a deck alone.
items.tweetA: Lyzr AI raised $100M using its own AI agent to run investor outreach, Q&A, and memo drafting. 4x oversubscribed. In a category (enterprise AI governance) where the whole pitch is 'trust our agents in production,' running the fundraise through your own agent is the only demo that actually proves the thesis.
items.tweetB: An immigrant founder who already built and sold one company (PowerUpCloud, 2019) used an AI agent to raise $100M for his next one. This isn't a vibe-coding story — it's what it looks like when a repeat founder with domain credibility lets the product do the selling instead of the deck.

charts.desc: No chart; round terms cited as text.

items.title: Hyperscaler capex growth rate is starting to stabilize — Ironsides is watching for the deceleration.
items.idea: Ironsides' 'Big Four Spenders' chart (the major hyperscaler capex trackers) shows the rate of change of capex starting to stabilize after a period of acceleration — flagged explicitly as a signal the firm is watching for a coming deceleration, without further elaboration in the free portion of the note.
items.angle: A stabilizing rate-of-change is the first derivative turning before the second — if this is right, it's an early tell for the hyperscaler capex deceleration that would directly stress the '25%+ of GDP growth is AI capex' dynamic flagged elsewhere this week (item #18).
items.tweetA: Rate of change of Big Tech capex is starting to stabilize, per Ironsides. That's the first derivative flattening before the second derivative turns negative. If AI capex really is >25% of US GDP growth right now, this is the earliest possible tell that the growth engine is about to downshift.
items.tweetB: Nobody trades 'rate of change is starting to stabilize' — but that's exactly the kind of unglamorous signal that shows up in a chart three months before it shows up in an earnings call. Filed under: watch, don't act, yet.

charts.desc: Figure 6: 'Isolating the Big Four Spenders' — capex rate-of-change chart, paywalled beyond the caption.

items.title: Oxford researchers built the first globally comparable framework for measuring which cities are actually most exposed to extreme heat.
items.idea: Frankopan flags a new Oxford paper (Kariyawasam, Khosla, Lizana) titled 'Moving beyond exposure: a globally comparable framework for heat risk assessment in cities,' which proposes a new model for assessing urban heat vulnerability beyond simple temperature-exposure metrics — comparing risk across cities on a standardized basis for the first time.
items.angle: A standardized cross-city heat-risk framework is exactly the kind of underlying methodology that eventually feeds into muni-bond climate-risk pricing, insurance underwriting, and real-estate risk models — worth tracking upstream of when it shows up in a rating agency's methodology.
items.tweetA: A new Oxford framework lets you compare heat risk across cities on a standardized basis for the first time — not just 'how hot' but genuinely comparable exposure scoring. This is the kind of paper that shows up in a muni-bond climate rating methodology in 18 months, quietly, with no headline attached.
items.tweetB: Climate risk pricing has been stuck on crude temperature-exposure proxies for years. A globally comparable heat-risk framework is the unsexy infrastructure work that eventually lets insurers and city planners actually price this properly instead of eyeballing it.

charts.desc: No chart; paywalled beyond the paper title and framing.

items.source: Stephen Innes / The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The GPIF yen-rescue narrative just died — $1.8T pension fund confirmed no allocation overhaul, USD/JPY through 162.
items.idea: Reports confirmed Japan has no immediate plans to overhaul the $1.8T Government Pension Investment Fund's asset allocation, killing the market's hoped-for 'structural yen buyer' narrative that had built after Finance Minister Katayama urged pension funds to invest more domestically. USD/JPY pushed through 162 on the news — a level Innes flags as where the risk/reward on further USD/JPY upside starts to turn, given Tokyo's history of intervening once positioning gets crowded and liquidity thins.
items.angle: The yen-bull case just lost its most credible structural pillar and reverted to pure carry-trade mechanics — which is a more dangerous, more crowded trade precisely because it's now unsupported by any pending institutional flow story.
items.tweetA: GPIF — $1.8 trillion — just confirmed no allocation overhaul is coming. The yen-bull thesis lost its one structural pillar and reverted to pure carry trade. USD/JPY through 162 on the news. Carry trades unsupported by institutional flow stories are exactly the setup Tokyo waits for before intervening.
items.tweetB: Political jawboning (Katayama: 'invest more at home') moved the yen more than an actual pension fund allocation change ever will — because GPIF's mandate is retiree returns, not currency defense, and that mandate doesn't bend to a finance minister's speech. The market briefly forgot that. Now it's remembering.

charts.desc: No chart; USD/JPY level and GPIF context in text.

items.title: SPX is 125 handles from its July target while momentum factors have already fallen 20+ points underneath it.
items.idea: Lord Fed's SPX 7,700 July target (set two weeks ago from 7,350) is nearly hit (SPX closed Friday at 7,575). But beneath the index: the mainstream momentum ETF fell 11% peak-to-trough, SOX went from 80% above its 200-day moving average to just 36-46% above it, and Mag7 corrected ~15% — all while VIX sits at a 16 handle suggesting an orderly market. His read: single-stock vol and positioning disagree sharply with what VIX is saying, and the next major move (he's now targeting 8,000) is more likely to be violent than smooth.
items.angle: A VIX-says-calm / dispersion-says-violent split is one of the more reliable setups for a volatility-selling unwind — worth checking single-name vol and skew directly rather than trusting the index-level VIX print this week.
items.tweetA: VIX at 16 says calm market. SOX went from 80% above its 200-DMA to 46%. Momentum ETFs fell 11% peak to trough. Mag7 corrected 15%. When the index-level vol print and the dispersion underneath disagree this much, the index print is the one lying to you.
items.tweetB: SPX target hit two weeks early. The next target is 8,000. But the author isn't calling a straight line there — he's calling both 8,000 AND a 5-10% air pocket before year-end, and admits he doesn't know which comes first. That's a more honest forecast than most 'here's my year-end target' notes manage.

charts.desc: No chart; SOX/200-DMA and momentum-ETF drawdown figures cited as text.

items.title: KOSPI just had its fastest 25% decline since the March 2020 COVID crash — while still trading 23% above its 200-day average.
items.idea: South Korea's KOSPI fell nearly 9% to start the week (SK Hynix down double digits despite a successful Friday ADR IPO), extending a 3-week decline that's now erased more than a quarter of the index's value — the fastest pace since the COVID crash. The genuinely strange part: even after this crash, KOSPI is still 23% above its 200-day moving average, an unusually extended reading for an index this far into bear-market territory.
items.angle: A crash this fast that still leaves the index 23% above trend is a market that was priced for perfection and is only now starting to unwind — the more relevant question isn't 'is this over' but 'how far back toward the 200-DMA does this have to go before it's a normal correction rather than a crash.'
items.tweetA: KOSPI: -25% in three weeks, the fastest since the March 2020 COVID crash. Also KOSPI: still 23% above its 200-day moving average. Those two facts together tell you this crash hasn't even gotten the index back to trend yet — it's unwinding an extension, not creating a new one.
items.tweetB: SK Hynix crashes double digits three days after a successful ADR IPO priced the euphoria. That sequencing — IPO pop, then crash — is the tell that the IPO itself was the top of positioning, not a validation of the story.

charts.desc: KOSPI 200-DMA spread chart since 1980 — shows how extended the index became before this crash, described but cut off by paywall.

items.title: SK Hynix -15%, Samsung -11%, both citing HBM pricing worries — while TSMC posts +36% quarterly sales beating elevated expectations.
items.idea: SK Hynix tumbled a record 15% and Samsung fell 11% after a Korea Investment & Securities report projected SK Hynix's operating profit may trail consensus by 8%, citing slower-than-expected pricing gains in high-bandwidth memory (HBM) specifically. The same morning, TSMC — the chipmaker for both Nvidia and Apple — reported a 36% jump in quarterly sales, meeting elevated expectations and signaling AI compute demand remains intact. Foreign investors sold $1.1B of KOSPI shares in a single session.
items.angle: This is a memory-specific selloff, not an AI-compute-demand selloff — TSMC's beat same-day is the tell that logic/compute demand is fine while HBM memory pricing is the actual weak link. Worth distinguishing memory-exposed names from compute-exposed names in the reaction.
items.tweetA: Same morning: SK Hynix -15%, Samsung -11% on HBM pricing worries. TSMC +36% quarterly sales, beat, AI demand 'intact.' This is not an AI-demand selloff, it's an HBM-memory-pricing selloff wearing an AI-crash costume. Compute names and memory names just diverged hard and most headlines aren't making that distinction.
items.tweetB: A Korean brokerage report projecting an 8% profit miss took 15% off SK Hynix and $1.1B out of KOSPI in one session. That's a wildly asymmetric reaction to one sell-side estimate revision — which tells you more about how crowded and fragile the positioning was than about the estimate itself.

charts.desc: No chart; price moves and sales figures cited as text.

items.title: Eurozone 2026 growth forecast cut to 0.5% from 0.7% — economists blame the Middle East reflare, not domestic demand.
items.idea: A Bloomberg poll of 56 economists (conducted July 3-8) cut 2026 eurozone growth forecasts to 0.5% from 0.7% a month earlier and 0.8% in the ECB's own baseline, explicitly citing renewed Middle East hostilities. Median inflation forecast ticked down slightly to 2.8% (still well above the ECB's 2% target) after oil prices partly reversed a sharp drop. Respondents expect another quarter-point hike in September, with the first cut not until September 2027.
items.angle: A geopolitical shock (Hormuz) is now showing up directly in consensus eurozone GDP forecasts within days — a faster transmission from Middle East headlines to European growth numbers than the standard 'oil price to CPI to rates' lag would suggest.
items.tweetA: Eurozone 2026 growth forecast: cut to 0.5% from 0.7%, explicitly blamed on Middle East hostilities, inside two weeks of the escalation. That's a much faster geopolitics-to-GDP-forecast transmission than the textbook oil-price-to-CPI-to-rates lag implies economists were using stale playbooks.
items.tweetB: ECB's own baseline: 0.8%. Independent economist consensus: 0.5%. That's a 30-basis-point gap between the central bank's official growth number and what the people polled by Bloomberg actually believe — worth remembering next time the ECB's forecast gets quoted as consensus.

charts.desc: No chart; poll figures cited as text.

items.title: China is targeting food self-sufficiency in the mid-90% range by 2035, up from ~60% today — even on high-cost land.
items.idea: Goldman's equity analysts, cited in Sethi's roundup, expect China's food self-sufficiency to rise to the mid-90% range by 2035 from today's low-60% level, consistent with Xi's repeated framing that 'the Chinese people's rice bowl should be firmly held in their own hands.' Much of this production happens on land that's higher-cost than Brazilian or US imports — meaning Beijing is explicitly willing to pay a cost premium for food security. India runs a parallel strategy via minimum support prices and strategic stockpiles.
items.angle: This is a direct, multi-decade demand floor under global grain and fertilizer markets that has nothing to do with price signals — China and India are both building strategic food buffers regardless of comparative-advantage economics, which changes the risk profile for ag-commodity shorts.
items.tweetA: China is targeting 90%+ food self-sufficiency by 2035, up from 60% today, on land that's more expensive than Brazilian or US imports. That's Beijing explicitly paying a cost premium for food security. If you're modeling global grain demand on comparative advantage, you're missing the actual variable driving Chinese ag policy.
items.tweetB: China and India are both building strategic food buffers right now — self-sufficiency targets and minimum support prices respectively — independent of what global grain prices are doing. That's a demand floor under ag commodities that doesn't show up in any supply/demand balance sheet until it's already priced in.

charts.desc: Goldman Exhibit 8 referenced: China food self-sufficiency trajectory to 2035, cited but not fully visible in email.

items.title: The market is sitting on its hands until hyperscaler earnings — even NVDA/META/AVGO strength isn't enough to force a move.
items.idea: SixSigmaCapital's Monday note: mega-caps (NVDA, META, AVGO) posted strong gains last week while software and healthcare were quiet, EM names showed relative strength, semis were mixed — the sector rotation thesis is 'only playing out in fits and starts.' His conclusion: with CPI/PPI this week and hyperscaler earnings not until end of month, he's not pressing new positions, just holding longs and waiting for setups.
items.angle: A professional PM explicitly saying 'the data this week (CPI/PPI) doesn't matter as much as data three weeks out (hyperscaler earnings)' is a useful positioning tell — it suggests the real catalyst risk for August isn't this week's macro prints at all.
items.tweetA: A PM's honest read going into a week with CPI, PPI, and bank earnings: none of it is the real catalyst. The real catalyst is hyperscaler earnings at month-end, and everything between now and then is noise to sit through, not trade. Worth remembering when CPI drops and Twitter loses its mind over a beat or miss that won't matter in three weeks.
items.tweetB: 'The rotation thesis is only playing out in fits and starts' is the most honest sentence in any positioning note this week. Mega-cap strength, quiet software, mixed semis — that's not a rotation, that's a market that hasn't decided what it wants to be yet.

charts.desc: Attached earnings-calendar image for week of July 13 — not embedded as inline chart.
